meta.name: Hyperventilation with Underlying Cause — Early DKA Masquerading as Panic Attack

patient.name: Madison Carver
patient.chiefComplaint: Rapid deep breathing for ~3 hours; feels anxious, nauseated, and 'can't catch a normal breath.' Coworker called 911 thinking she was having a panic attack.

history.hpi: 24-year-old female with longstanding Type 1 diabetes (diagnosed age 12) who has been feeling progressively unwell for the last two days — frequent urination, constant thirst, fatigue, and decreased appetite. Last night she slept poorly and skipped her insulin pump basal because she 'wasn't eating' and the pump site felt itchy. This morning at the bookstore where she works, coworkers noticed she was sweating, breathing very fast and deep, and looked anxious. She reports nausea, intermittent crampy mid-abdominal pain, mild lightheadedness, and a sensation she 'can't get a deep enough breath in,' though she denies chest pain, cough, wheezing, leg swelling, fever, or sick contacts. She has not vomited yet today. She has felt 'high' on her finger-stick glucose readings the last two days (mid-300s) but hasn't given correction boluses because she thought maybe the pump or sensor was wrong. Her insulin pump infusion set is visible on her abdomen but looks slightly crusted at the entry point. She does not feel like she is having a panic attack — 'this feels different from those.'
pastMedical: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus since age 12 (insulin pump x4 years)
Generalized anxiety disorder — no recent panic attacks; last seen by psychiatrist 6 months ago
medications: Insulin aspart via insulin pump (basal + meal boluses) — basal suspended overnight per patient
history.lastOralIntake: A few sips of water this morning; last solid food was dinner ~14 hours ago (light salad, did not finish it).
history.events: Felt unwell with polyuria/polydipsia x2 days → skipped overnight basal insulin (pump site itchy, not eating) → woke fatigued → at work coworkers noticed rapid deep breathing, sweating, anxious-looking presentation → coworker called 911 reporting 'panic attack.'

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 24-year-old female, possible panic attack, hyperventilating, conscious and talking. Retail business.
scene.location: Small independent bookstore, back office. Patient seated in a desk chair, leaning slightly forward, hands resting on the desk. Good lighting; clear access. Coworker present.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Coworker is calm and helpful; no behavioral threat. Aisles and exits clear.
scene.bystanders: Female coworker reports patient 'has been breathing weird all morning,' looks 'really stressed,' and 'mentioned she didn't take her insulin last night.' Coworker has known patient for two years and says 'she's anxious sometimes but it doesn't usually look like this.'
visualCues: Young adult female, alert, sitting upright in a chair
Visibly rapid, DEEP, regular breaths — chest wall rising fully each breath, NOT shallow panicky breathing
Mild diaphoresis on forehead; flushed cheeks; lips slightly dry
Insulin pump clipped to waistband; infusion set tubing visible into the abdomen
Half-finished water bottle on desk; no food
MedicAlert bracelet on left wrist: 'TYPE 1 DIABETES'
No tripod posture, no accessory muscle use, no audible wheeze, no stridor
If a provider gets close to the patient's face — faintly sweet/fruity (acetone) breath odor

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS arrives at the bookstore back office to find a 24-year-old female sitting in a desk chair, alert, anxious-appearing, breathing rapidly and deeply. Coworker reports a possible panic attack. The picture is deceptive: respirations are FAST and DEEP (Kussmaul), not shallow, and SpO2 is normal at 99% — she is not actually hypoxic or short of breath in the traditional sense. She has Type 1 diabetes with an insulin pump, skipped her overnight basal, polyuria/polydipsia x2 days, mild abdominal pain, faintly fruity breath, and a MedicAlert bracelet visible. This is the assessment window. The teaching point: a complete primary survey, targeted history, breath odor, and a FINGER-STICK GLUCOSE — not a paper bag — is what discriminates DKA from panic attack. Crews that anchor on 'panic attack' and skip glucose will go down the wrong-turn branch.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent — speaking in full sentences, no stridor, no drooling
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 32, DEEP and regular (Kussmaul pattern); chest wall rises fully each breath; NO accessory muscle use, NO retractions, NO prolonged expiratory phase, lung sounds CLEAR bilaterally
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular radial pulse, slightly fast; warm/dry-to-slightly-moist skin; cap refill 2 sec; mucous membranes slightly dry
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Symmetric, intact, follows commands, moves all extremities
otherFindings: Lung sounds CLEAR bilaterally in all fields — no wheezes, no crackles, no diminished air entry
Capnography (if applied): normal waveform shape, EtCO2 LOW at 22 mmHg (compensatory hyperventilation blowing off CO2 to buffer metabolic acidosis)
Faintly sweet/fruity (acetone) breath odor when close to the patient's face
Dry mucous membranes; dry-cracked lips; sunken-appearing eyes
Insulin pump visible at left waistband; infusion site on left lower abdomen looks slightly red/crusted
Mild diffuse mid-abdominal tenderness on palpation; no rebound, no guarding
MedicAlert bracelet: 'TYPE 1 DIABETES'
Patient denies tingling around mouth/fingertips (would be expected with primary hyperventilation/panic)
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I can't slow it down. I've been peeing all day and so thirsty. My stomach hurts. I didn't take my insulin last night. I don't think this is a panic attack.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey (ABC); note Kussmaul pattern, clear lungs, normal SpO2 — NOT primary respiratory failure
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Care Goals + Pertinent Assessment — recognize that deep, rapid breathing with clear lungs, normal SpO2, and low EtCO2 is compensatory hyperventilation for metabolic acidosis, not a primary respiratory problem.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Care Goals: Assure adequate oxygenation and ventilation. Recognize impending respiratory failure. Promptly identify and intervene for patients who require escalation of therapy. Deliver appropriate therapy by differentiating likely cause of respiratory distress. Alleviate respiratory distress.

expectedActions.shortAction: Targeted history: onset, prior episodes, diabetes, insulin compliance, sick days, urinary frequency, thirst
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Inclusion Criteria 4 + Educational 3 I's — polyuria, polydipsia, tachypnea in a known diabetic with missed insulin is a textbook DKA-trigger history. This step IS the assessment that uncovers the underlying cause.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Inclusion Criteria: Adult or pediatric patient with symptoms of hyperglycemia (e.g., polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, dizziness, abdominal pain, tachypnea). Adult or pediatric patient with history of diabetes and other medical symptoms. Notes/Educational Pearls — Key Considerations: Consider causes for hyperglycemia by thinking about the 3 I's: Insulin: This refers to any medication changes for insulin or oral medications including poor compliance or malfunctioning insulin pump. Ischemia: This refers to hyperglycemia sometimes being an indication of physiologic stress in a patient and can be a clue to myocardial ischemia in particular. Infection: Underlying infection can cause derangements in glucose control.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check finger-stick blood glucose — THIS is the discriminator (will read 480 mg/dL)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Assessment 1a + Altered Mental Status Assessment 9 + Performance Measures — point-of-care glucose is the single discriminating test that distinguishes panic attack from DKA. Any patient with tachypnea, AMS, or symptoms of hyperglycemia gets a glucose. Skipping this is the most common error in this presentation.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Assessment — Monitoring: Check blood glucose level. Performance Measures: When in scope of practice, point of care blood glucose checked for all patients with symptoms of altered level of consciousness, seizure, stroke, or hyperglycemia.

expectedActions.shortAction: Assess breath odor (acetone/fruity = DKA); auscultate lungs (clear → not bronchospasm)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Pertinent Assessment 2 + AMS Assessment 11 — fruity breath and Kussmaul respirations are the classic DKA bedside findings; breath odor distinguishes metabolic causes (acidosis, alcohol, uremia) when AMS is on the differential.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pertinent Assessment Findings: Concomitant trauma. Abdominal pain, 'fruity breath,' and rapid-deep respirations (Kussmaul respirations) may be associated with DKA.

expectedActions.shortAction: Secondary survey: dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, abdominal exam, skin turgor
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Assessment 2 — secondary survey pertinent to altered glucose looks specifically at dehydration markers (sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes), abdominal pain (common in pediatric/new DKA), tachycardia, hypotension, and tachypnea.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Secondary survey pertinent to altered blood glucose level: Constitutional: assess for tachycardia, hypotension, and tachypnea. Eyes: assess for sunken eyes from dehydration. Nose/mouth/ears: assess for dry mucous membranes or tongue bite from seizure. Abdominal pain including nausea and vomiting especially in children. Neurologic: Assess Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) and mental status. Assess for focal neurologic deficit: motor and sensory.

expectedActions.shortAction: Apply SpO2 + waveform capnography; document baseline (low EtCO2 ≈ 22 confirms compensation)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Monitoring 2a + Patient Safety 1 — pulse oximetry and EtCO2 are routine in respiratory complaints; a LOW EtCO2 with tachypnea and clear lungs supports compensatory hyperventilation rather than bronchospasm or impending respiratory failure.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Monitoring: Pulse oximetry and EtCO2 should be routinely used as adjuncts to other forms of monitoring in patients with respiratory complaints. Patient Safety Considerations: Normal EtCO2 (35–45 mmHg) with tachypnea and respiratory distress is an indicator of impending respiratory failure.

expectedActions.shortAction: Supplemental O2 only if SpO2 < 94% (here 99% on RA — no O2 needed)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Treatment 1a — supplemental O2 titrated to 94–98%; this patient is already 99% so routine O2 is not indicated and could mask oxygenation trend.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Airway: Give supplemental oxygen for dyspnea to a target of 94–98% saturation. Escalate from a nasal cannula as needed to reach this goal.

transitions.condition: Crew performs a proper primary and secondary survey, takes a targeted history (diabetes, missed insulin, polyuria/polydipsia, abdominal pain), assesses breath odor, and obtains a finger-stick glucose reading of 480 mg/dL. They recognize DKA as the underlying cause of the Kussmaul respirations and treat the metabolic problem, not the breathing pattern.

transitions.if: Treated as panic attack; no glucose checked
transitions.condition: Crew accepts the dispatch label of 'panic attack' and coaches the patient through slow breathing or has her breathe into a paper bag to 'rebreathe CO2.' Glucose is NOT obtained. The CO2 rebreathing reverses the patient's compensatory hyperventilation, raises PaCO2, and drops pH further — the metabolic acidosis worsens and the patient deteriorates.

phases.name: DKA recognized; IV + fluids started
phases.description: Crew has identified the underlying cause: early diabetic ketoacidosis with compensatory Kussmaul respirations. Glucose reads 480 mg/dL, patient is mildly dehydrated, hemodynamics borderline (HR 118, BP 108/68), normal SpO2. Treatment now follows the Hyperglycemia Guideline: establish IV access, begin a normal saline bolus at the protocol rate (20 mL/kg at 1000 mL/hr → for 64 kg, 1280 mL total goal), obtain a 12-lead EKG to screen for hyperkalemia or ACS, place the patient on cardiac monitor, and prepare for transport. Insulin and bicarbonate are NOT given prehospital. The teaching point in this phase is correct prehospital DKA management — fluids + monitor + transport, not insulin or bicarb.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew recognized the metabolic cause of the Kussmaul respirations after obtaining a finger-stick glucose of 480 mg/dL, identifying classic DKA history (T1DM, missed insulin, polyuria/polydipsia, abd pain, fruity breath), and is now treating per the Hyperglycemia Guideline rather than the Respiratory Distress Guideline.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 30, deep regular (Kussmaul); lungs clear; SpO2 99% on room air; EtCO2 23
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular pulses; warm extremities; cap refill 2 sec; IV established in L AC with 18g; NS infusing wide-open through pressure bag

skin.moisture: mildly diaphoretic, mucous membranes still dry

otherFindings: Lungs remain clear; no change in respiratory pattern yet (acidosis will not correct prehospital)
12-lead obtained: sinus tachycardia, no peaked T waves, no STEMI
Patient appears slightly less anxious now that crew has a plan and is talking through it
Insulin pump suspended per crew/patient decision in consult with online medical control
Nausea improving; no vomiting
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Thank you for listening. I really didn't think this was a panic attack.

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish IV access (18g AC if able); place on cardiac monitor
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 2a + Shock Treatment 8 — IV access is required for fluid resuscitation in symptomatic hyperglycemia with dehydration markers; cardiac monitor for arrhythmia screening.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: If glucose greater than 250 mg/dL with symptoms of dehydration, vomiting, abdominal pain, or altered level of consciousness: Provide volume expansion with normal saline bolus.

expectedActions.shortAction: Normal saline 1280 mL bolus IV (64 kg × 20 mL/kg) at 1000 mL/hr; reassess
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 2a-i — adult symptomatic hyperglycemia gets NS 20 mL/kg at 1000 mL/hr. For 64 kg this is 1280 mL total goal. Closely watch for signs of fluid overload or cerebral edema (rare in adults).
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If glucose greater than 250 mg/dL with symptoms of dehydration, vomiting, abdominal pain, or altered level of consciousness: Provide volume expansion with normal saline bolus. Adult: Normal saline 20 mL/kg at rate of 1000 mL/hr; if symptoms of hypovolemic shock, follow Shock Guideline.

expectedActions.shortAction: Obtain 12-lead EKG — screen for hyperkalemia (peaked T) and ACS
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Assessment 4 — 12-lead EKG to assess for hyperkalemia or ACS in any symptomatic hyperglycemic patient.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Obtain 12-lead EKG to assess for findings consistent with hyperkalemia or acute coronary syndrome.

expectedActions.shortAction: Evaluate for concomitant sepsis/infection (temperature, infusion-site exam, recent illness)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Assessment 3 + Educational 3 I's (Infection) — infection is a common DKA trigger; in this patient the slightly red insulin pump site is suspicious and should be documented.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Evaluate for possible concomitant sepsis and septic shock. Notes/Educational Pearls — Infection: Underlying infection can cause derangements in glucose control.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess vitals, mental status, and recheck glucose every 15 min en route
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 5 — reassess vitals, mental status, hydration; if mental status changes, recheck glucose to rule out iatrogenic hypoglycemia (irrelevant here since no insulin is given prehospital).
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Reassess patient: Reassess vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment), mental status, and signs of dehydration. If mental status changes, reassess blood glucose level and provide appropriate treatment if hypoglycemia has developed.

expectedActions.shortAction: Do NOT give bicarbonate empirically; do NOT push insulin prehospital
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 4 + Patient Safety 1-2 — bicarbonate is reserved for documented hyperkalemia; insulin therapy belongs at the receiving facility. Overly aggressive interventions can cause harm (cerebral edema, hyponatremia, hypokalemia).
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If findings of hyperkalemia, consider administration of sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg (max dose of 50 mEq) IV bolus over 5 minutes and consider albuterol 5 mg via nebulizer (can be repeated if no response is seen). Patient Safety Considerations: Overly aggressive administration of fluid in hyperglycemic patients may cause cerebral edema or dangerous hyponatremia. Asymptomatic hyperglycemia poses no risk to the patient while inappropriately aggressive interventions to manage blood sugar may harm patients.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to closest appropriate ED with continued NS, monitor, and reassessment
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 6a — disposition is transport to the closest appropriate receiving facility for definitive insulin and electrolyte management.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Disposition: Transport to closest appropriate receiving facility.

transitions.if: Fluids running and transporting to ED
transitions.condition: Crew has IV access, NS bolus running at protocol rate, 12-lead obtained, patient on monitor, and is transporting. En-route reassessment confirms hemodynamic stability and unchanged mental status.

phases.name: Transport; stable en route
phases.description: En route to the ED. NS bolus has infused approximately 600 mL of the 1280 mL goal. Patient remains alert, oriented, and cooperative. Kussmaul respirations persist as expected — acidosis will not correct without insulin at the receiving facility — but HR is trending down, BP is stable, glucose remains in the high 400s, and mental status is unchanged. Crew gives a clean radio report including the deceptive presentation (looked like panic attack at first), the key discriminator (finger-stick glucose 480), the working diagnosis (DKA), and treatment so far. Bedside handoff at the ED uses a structured format (SBAR or similar) and the patient is transitioned to the ED bed with continued NS, monitor, and pump suspended.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew is transporting with appropriate DKA management in progress. Hemodynamics are stable, mental status is unchanged, and a structured handoff to the receiving facility is anticipated.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 28, deep regular; lungs clear; SpO2 99%; EtCO2 24
clinicalPresentation.circulation: HR 108, BP 114/72, warm pink extremities, strong pulses; NS ~600 mL infused via pressure infusion

skin.moisture: less diaphoretic; mucous membranes slightly improved

otherFindings: Patient reports nausea is better; abdominal pain still present but tolerable
Repeat glucose 462 mg/dL (essentially unchanged — expected, no insulin given)
Patient remains GCS 15; no change in mental status
Insulin pump remains suspended
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Breathing still feels weird, but I feel a little less awful.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess vitals, mental status, glucose, and dehydration signs q15 min en route
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 5a — serial reassessment is required; documents response to fluids and detects deterioration early.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Reassess patient: Reassess vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment), mental status, and signs of dehydration.

expectedActions.shortAction: Monitor for AMS, headache, ICP signs — stop fluids and elevate head if cerebral edema suspected
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Patient Safety 1 — cerebral edema is rare in adults but is the leading cause of DKA death in children; crew must recognize the warning signs and act.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Overly aggressive administration of fluid in hyperglycemic patients may cause cerebral edema or dangerous hyponatremia. Cerebral edema is a leading cause of death in children with DKA but is very rare in adults. Closely monitor for signs of altered mental status, increased intracranial pressure, and immediately discontinue IV fluids and elevate head of bed if signs of increased ICP develop. Reassess and manage airway as needed.

expectedActions.shortAction: Structured ED handoff: pertinent history, glucose 480, DKA, fluids given, deceptive presentation
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Key Documentation + Performance Measures — document reassessment, glucose, fluids given; structured handoff prevents loss of the cognitive thread of 'looked like a panic attack but wasn't.'
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Document reassessment of vital signs and mental status after administration of IV fluids. Document glucose level (if in scope of practice) when indicated.

phases.name: Treated as panic attack; acidosis worsening
phases.description: Crew accepted the dispatch label and the surface presentation. They coached slow breathing or had the patient breathe into a paper bag to 'rebreathe CO2.' No finger-stick glucose was checked. Coaching/rebreathing has the opposite of the intended effect: it raises the patient's PaCO2, which removes her compensatory hyperventilation, drops her arterial pH further, and worsens the metabolic acidosis. Over a few minutes she becomes more lethargic, her speech slows, her respirations become slightly more shallow (no longer fully compensating), she vomits once, and her mental status deteriorates from GCS 15 to GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5). This is the wrong-turn branch — the teaching point is that anchoring on a dispatch label without doing the assessment causes harm.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew accepted dispatch's 'panic attack' label, coached the patient through slow breathing or had her breathe into a paper bag to rebreathe CO2, and did not obtain a finger-stick glucose. Compensatory hyperventilation is reversed, PaCO2 rises, arterial pH falls, and the metabolic acidosis worsens.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent for now; protective reflexes intact but mentation declining
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 22, shallower than baseline (no longer fully compensating); lungs clear; SpO2 96%; EtCO2 has RISEN to 32 mmHg
clinicalPresentation.circulation: HR 124, BP 102/64; cool peripheries developing; cap refill 3 sec
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Symmetric but slower; obeys commands when prompted but eyes drift closed when unstimulated
otherFindings: Patient just vomited a small amount of clear/yellow fluid
Speech now slow and confused: knows her name, vaguely knows the date, intermittently confuses where she is
Coworker says: 'She's worse than when you got here'
Insulin pump and MedicAlert bracelet still visible — student has another chance to notice
If glucose is finally checked now: 488 mg/dL — DKA is now obvious
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Sleepy … (drifts off, opens eyes when name called) … just want to … rest.

expectedActions.shortAction: Stop CO2 rebreathing immediately; reassess airway, breathing, mental status
expectedActions.rationale: MATC AMS Patient Safety 1 — once mental status worsens during a presumed-panic intervention, the priority is airway protection, oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion. CO2 rebreathing in a compensating acidotic patient is the cause of decline and must be stopped.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: With depressed mental status, initial focus is on airway protection, oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check finger-stick glucose NOW; treat the underlying cause, not the breathing pattern
expectedActions.rationale: MATC AMS Assessment 9 + Performance Measures — point-of-care glucose is mandatory in any patient with altered mental status. This is the bailout for the wrong-turn branch.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Look for treatable causes of altered mental status (AMS): Blood glucose level. Performance Measures: Hypoglycemia considered and treated appropriately: Blood glucose level obtained.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize DKA: T1DM + missed insulin + polyuria + Kussmaul + glucose 488 + fruity breath
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Inclusion 1 + Pertinent Assessment 2 — the picture is now unmistakable. Cross to the Hyperglycemia Guideline and start fluids.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Inclusion Criteria: Adult or pediatric patient with altered level of consciousness. Adult or pediatric patient with symptoms of hyperglycemia (e.g., polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, dizziness, abdominal pain, tachypnea). Pertinent Assessment Findings: Abdominal pain, 'fruity breath,' and rapid-deep respirations (Kussmaul respirations) may be associated with DKA.

expectedActions.shortAction: Manage airway: position, suction available, supplemental O2 to keep SpO2 ≥ 94%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC AMS Treatment 1 + Patient Safety 1 — protect the airway in a patient now GCS 12 with a recent emesis; have suction ready and elevate head.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Oxygen. Patient Safety Considerations: With depressed mental status, initial focus is on airway protection, oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion.

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish IV; start NS 1280 mL bolus (64 kg × 20 mL/kg) at 1000 mL/hr
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 2a-i — adult with glucose > 250 + symptoms of dehydration/AMS gets a NS bolus at the protocol rate; this is the recovery path back onto the default treatment.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If glucose greater than 250 mg/dL with symptoms of dehydration, vomiting, abdominal pain, or altered level of consciousness: Provide volume expansion with normal saline bolus. Adult: Normal saline 20 mL/kg at rate of 1000 mL/hr; if symptoms of hypovolemic shock, follow Shock Guideline.

expectedActions.shortAction: Screen for shock (BP, HR, cap refill, lactate if available) — treat per Shock Guideline if criteria met
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Hyperglycemia Treatment 1 + Shock Guideline — once perfusion markers are abnormal (HR 124, cap refill 3 sec, cool peripheries), cross-check shock criteria; DKA can co-exist with septic shock and the trigger for this presentation may be infection at the pump site.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Inclusion Criteria: Signs of poor perfusion (due to a medical cause) such as one or more of the following: Altered mental status. Delayed capillary refill (> 3 seconds). Tachycardia or bradycardia for age, out of proportion to temperature. Cool/mottled or flushed/ruddy skin.

transitions.if: Recognize error; check glucose; begin DKA care
transitions.condition: Crew stops the rebreathing, finally obtains a finger-stick glucose, recognizes DKA, and crosses to the Hyperglycemia Guideline (IV access, NS bolus, monitor, transport). The patient stabilizes hemodynamically and mental status recovers as the compensatory hyperventilation resumes and fluids are infused.
